summary:
The Battle of Mogadishu on October 3–4, 1993, known popularly as Black Hawk Down after the title of Mark Bowden's 1999 book, was an engagement between US Army Rangers and Delta Force operators and Somali militia loyal to warlord Mohamed Farrah Aidid. A mission to capture senior Aidid lieutenants in the Bakara Market district of Mogadishu went catastrophically wrong when Somali forces shot down two Black Hawk helicopters. American forces fought through the night to defend the crash sites and their wounded, and rescue convoys struggled to reach them through ambushed streets.

Eighteen American soldiers were killed and 73 wounded; Somali casualties were far higher, with estimates ranging from 300 to over 1,000 killed. Images of dead American soldiers being dragged through Mogadishu's streets shocked the US public. President Clinton withdrew American forces from Somalia within months. The battle had a profound influence on US military and political willingness to intervene in African conflicts: the perceived lesson — that the US could not sustain casualties in humanitarian interventions — was directly cited as a reason for American reluctance to intervene in the [[event:2695|Rwandan Genocide]] that occurred the following year.
